source.title: Hold-and-release surge log (microburst capture)
content: Context: quick personal run to capture hold-and-release microbursts we keep seeing in customer traces. Trying to reproduce the pattern where a queue builds quietly and then a flood of requests is released (client-side batching) causing transient tail blowups.
Goal: map p50/p95/p99 behavior, identify whether KV-cache warmup or batching thresholding is the dominant penalty, and sanity-check backoff heuristics.
Date of runs: 2025-12-02 to 2026-02-10 (iterative). This doc collates the most representative traces.
Environment:
- cluster: dev-fleet-us-west-2 (mixed A100 + H100), pinned to 1x A100 per worker for these runs
- model: opt-13b-quant (4-bit q4m) served via runtime v2.3.1
- runtime flags: continuous-batching=true, preemption=off, kv-cache=true
- client: surge-runner v0.9 (local load injector), clients=48, connection multiplexing disabled
- target route: /v1/generate (no function-calls), prompt length ~128 tok avg, gen length target 64
Pattern injected (hold-and-release):
- hold period: 18s of steady low qps (1-2 qps sustained) to allow many clients to queue locally
- release pulse: 1s ramp to peak (~450 concurrent requests), then exponential decay 1.5s half-life
- repeat cadence: every 60s x 10 pulses per run
Experiment knobs varied across runs:
- batching timeout: {2ms, 6ms, 12ms}
- max batch size: {32, 64, 128}
- kv-cache priming: (cold, primed with synthetic prefixes), eviction-policy: LRU vs fractional-reserve
- arrival smoothing: client-side hold jitter applied vs none
Representative run summary (best reproducible run):
- config: batching_timeout=6ms, max_batch=64, kv-cache primed, client jitter 30ms
- measured overall peak qps: 430 (server-side accepted), sustained during pulse ~380 qps
- p50: 18ms, p90: 72ms, p95: 128ms, p99: 540ms, max observed: 2.2s (few outliers)
- GPU util during pulse: 85% (avg), but tailing tasks show GPU context-switch spikes
- queue depth observed on worker: instantaneous queue=220 at pulse peak
- cache hit rate on warm run: 78% (requests sharing prefixes), cold run: 18%
- eviction spikes: when queue depth > 150, observed small burst of KV-evictions triggering recompute overhead
Latency histogram (ASCII rough):
Observations / quick hypotheses:

1) Primary source of p99 inflation is not GPU saturation alone — GPU util near 85% but still has headroom; rather, queueing + batch-coalescing + KV-cache eviction interplay creates long-tail recompute/IOs.

2) When batching_timeout is short (2ms) we see more small batches, higher scheduling overhead, and slightly higher p50 but slightly lower p99 — because large queueing collapses into fewer big batches that cause evictions.
3) When batching_timeout is long (12ms) we see fewer but bigger batches; p50 improves but p99 worsens dramatically due to long service times for huge batches and longer KV eviction windows.

4) KV-cache priming matters a lot: cold runs have many cache-miss recomputes that cascade during a pulse. Eviction-policy LRU reduces p99 compared to fractional-reserve in my tests (surprising — need to confirm).
5) Client-side release jitter (30-50ms) smooths the pulse and reduces p99 by ~35% in these synthetic runs. Not a panacea but inexpensive.
Representative trace notes (timestamps relative to pulse start):
- t=0s: clients release, queue ramps 0->220 over 350ms
- t=0.35s: first big batch scheduled, batch size ~112, single-model forward time jumps, downstream latency amplification starts
- t=0.8s: eviction batch observed, 18% of requests triggered recompute (cache-miss), ~700ms extra per request in that subset
- t=1.6s: system drains to backpressure equilibrium, tail decays but a few requests still executing (1-2s)
Notes on instrumentation gaps:
- need per-request cache-miss tagging surfaced in trace logs (currently requires correlating eviction counters with request ps).
- scheduling timestamps are coarse (10ms buckets) — hard to isolate whether scheduling or model forward is dominant in some spikes.
Actionable next steps (short list):
- (A) Add a per-request KV hit/miss flag in the runtime logs for easier correlation.
- (B) Run the same hold-release pattern but with small global pre-warming (send short priming prefixes 10s before release) to test partial-priming effectiveness.
- (C) Try adaptive batching: dynamically cap batch size during pulse if estimated queue depth > 120, compare p99.
- (D) Validate that eviction-policy LRU wins across more seeds and on H100 nodes.
- (E) Prototype client-side soft-release (stochastic release over 300ms) as a low-effort mitigation for PLG clients.
Notes-to-self / TODOs:
- capture full flame graphs for the worst 50 p99 requests next run (attach to INF-3247).
- coordinate with Samir to add cache-miss flag in logs (ticket created).
- try lowering model concurrency target (threads-per-device) to see if that reduces scheduling jitter.
Final quick takeaways (for README/notes):
- Hold-and-release patterns produce microbursts that are mainly harmful because of stateful caches and batch-size amplification, not purely because of peak QPS.
- Mitigations should be multi-pronged: client-side smoothing, runtime per-batch anti-amplification heuristics, and better cache priming signals.
- Low-hanging wins: client jitter + small priming window reduced p99 materially in these runs — worth surfaced guidance for customers and an SDK helper.
Appendix: raw sample metrics (CSV-ish lines) for the representative run:
timestamp,qps,p50_ms,p95_ms,p99_ms,queue_depth,gpu_util,kv_hit_pct
(end of doc)

source.title: trailing-punct-hallucination-hunch-mateo
source.content:
Quick hunch / scratchpad (late-night).

Summary:
- Observing a disproportionate number of hallucinations when prompts end with trailing punctuation or incomplete sentence terminators. Example: prompts that finish with '...' or a trailing ':' or '-' sometimes trigger fabricated facts or continuation tokens that look like instructions.
- Pattern surfaced in small-sample A/B vs. baseline on hosted-api (model: rdinfer-small-2025-preview). Rate ~+6% problematic for short queries (<12 tokens).

Why this matters:
- Short UI queries (search-asst, quick-fact) are common product surface; even small regression increases user trust issues.
- Might be related to tokenization boundary or early KV cache misalignment on short sequences.

Observed examples (raw, unfiltered):
1) Prompt: 'Who is the CEO of Redwood Inference...'
   Response: 'The CEO of Redwood Inference is Jonathan Meyers, previously at CloudWays, who led the Series B in 2022.'
   Notes: company CEO is not Jonathan Meyers (fabricated last employer + fundraising detail).

2) Prompt: 'When was Redwood Inference founded:'
   Response: 'It was founded in April 2018 by a team from Stanford and Berkeley.'
   Notes: date and founding details invented; baseline model without trailing ':' returned safe reply ('I don't have that info' or accurate year).

3) Prompt: 'What is the recommended batch size for 8k context -'
   Response: 'Use batch size 256 and 8-bit quantization; ensure to set KV cache reset to 5 tokens.'
   Notes: concrete numbers fabricated; could be dangerous if surfaced to ops docs.

Ad-hoc scoring rubric (mini quickscore for triage) - 0..3 scale:
- 0: Correct or defensible (minor phrasing). No fabricated entities or dates.
- 1: Minor hallucination (small extra detail, low-risk). e.g., guessed title but not harmful.
- 2: Fabricated factual claim (person, date, event) not grounded in prompt. Medium risk.
- 3: Dangerous/authoritative hallucination (fabricated contact info, security claims, instructions) high risk.

Sample scoring (from above):
- Example 1 -> 2
- Example 2 -> 2
- Example 3 -> 3 (could mislead infra ops)

Repro steps (quick):
1) Run the short-query harness: tests/mini_trailing_punct/ with seed=42, 2k samples of short prompts (randomized endings: '.', '...', ':', '-', '?' and no punctuation).
2) Compare model variants: rdinfer-2025-base, rdinfer-small-2025-preview (the one showing regression), rdinfer-small-2025-control.
3) Collect token-level traces and KV cache dump for sessions where score >=2.
4) Flag samples where reply contains named entities + dates + numeric specifics.

Hypotheses (ranked):
- H1: Tokenizer yields different trailing token IDs for '...' and ':' which interact with position encodings in a way that biases next-token distribution toward proper-noun heavy continuations.
- H2: KV cache reuse bug: short prompts with non-terminating punctuation don't trigger cache flush leading to bleed from previous requests in pooled workers.
- H3: Sampling/temperature default changed in preview build (compare logprobs to baseline).
- H4: Prompt normalization pipeline (strip/normalize punctuation) differs between serving runtime versions.

Quick checks to disambiguate H1/H2/H3:
- Run same prompt in single-tenant dedicated pool (no worker reuse). If rate drops -> H2.
- Force tokenization debug output (show tokens for trailing chars); if token pattern differs from baseline -> H1.
- Run with temperature=0 and deterministic decoding; if hallucination persists -> less likely sampling noise (H3 less likely).

Mitigation ideas (short-term):
- Pre-normalize user-facing short queries: strip trailing '...' and lone ':' or '-' before passing to model. Quick but affects UX.
- Add a post-filter that downgrades/blocks replies containing newly invented PERSON/ORG/DATE triples for <15-token prompts.
- Force KV cache reset for sessions under 16 tokens in the preview build.

Next steps / TODO (me / team):
- [ ] Create mini harness + baseline vs preview comparison. (target: this week)
- [ ] Reproduce on dedicated pool to check worker reuse. (ask ops: Asha)
- [ ] Add tokenization debug output to lightweight run. (Evelyn to help)
- [ ] If repro, open high-priority runtime bug with release-engineering; include KV dumps. (RED-4321 referenced)

Notes to self / messy hypotheses:
- Could be related to continuous batching edge-case: when a request ends with punctuation the runtime batcher sometimes concatenates a tiny padding token sequence? Haven't confirmed.
- Seen similar behavior in past when we changed default eos handling (2024-09). Check release note.

Questions for sync with Applied ML:
- Have we changed tokenization config or tokenizer model since last stable release?
- Can we run tokenization of the exact strings across variants quickly?

Slack refs / links (placeholders):
- slack: #applied-ml thread 'trailing-punct-hunch' (TBD)
- linear: RED-4321 (linked above)

If I get one quick win: add a small preprocessor that trims repeated trailing punctuation for short queries and ship as a toggle for hosted-api. Low effort, reduces surface while we dig deeper.

End notes: store a few more raw samples (save to /tmp/trailing_punct_samples.json) and run small annotation round (3 raters) to validate rubric consistency.

-- Mateo
(half-baked, will clean after repro)

- Example 2 -> 2
- Example 3 -> 3 (could mislead infra ops)

Repro steps (quick):
1) Run the short-query harness: tests/mini_trailing_punct/ with seed=42, 2k samples of short prompts (randomized endings: '.', '...', ':', '-', '?' and no punctuation).
2) Compare model variants: rdinfer-2025-base, rdinfer-small-2025-preview (the one showing regression), rdinfer-small-2025-control.
3) Collect token-level traces and KV cache dump for sessions where score >=2.
4) Flag samples where reply contains named entities + dates + numeric specifics.

Hypotheses (ranked):
- H1: Tokenizer yields different trailing token IDs for '...' and ':' which interact with position encodings in a way that biases next-token distribution toward proper-noun heavy continuations.
- H2: KV cache reuse bug: short prompts with non-terminating punctuation don't trigger cache flush leading to bleed from previous requests in pooled workers.
- H3: Sampling/temperature default changed in preview build (compare logprobs to baseline).
- H4: Prompt normalization pipeline (strip/normalize punctuation) differs between serving runtime versions.

Quick checks to disambiguate H1/H2/H3:
- Run same prompt in single-tenant dedicated pool (no worker reuse). If rate drops -> H2.
- Force tokenization debug output (show tokens for trailing chars); if token pattern differs from baseline -> H1.
- Run with temperature=0 and deterministic decoding; if hallucination persists -> less likely sampling noise (H3 less likely).

Mitigation ideas (short-term):
- Pre-normalize user-facing short queries: strip trailing '...' and lone ':' or '-' before passing to model. Quick but affects UX.
- Add a post-filter that downgrades/blocks replies containing newly invented PERSON/ORG/DATE triples for <15-token prompts.
- Force KV cache reset for sessions under 16 tokens in the preview build.

Next steps / TODO (me / team):
- [ ] Create mini harness + baseline vs preview comparison. (target: this week)
- [ ] Reproduce on dedicated pool to check worker reuse. (ask ops: Asha)
- [ ] Add tokenization debug output to lightweight run. (Evelyn to help)
- [ ] If repro, open high-priority runtime bug with release-engineering; include KV dumps. (RED-4321 referenced)

Notes to self / messy hypotheses:
- Could be related to continuous batching edge-case: when a request ends with punctuation the runtime batcher sometimes concatenates a tiny padding token sequence? Haven't confirmed.
- Seen similar behavior in past when we changed default eos handling (2024-09). Check release note.

Questions for sync with Applied ML:
- Have we changed tokenization config or tokenizer model since last stable release?
- Can we run tokenization of the exact strings across variants quickly?

Slack refs / links (placeholders):
- slack: #applied-ml thread 'trailing-punct-hunch' (TBD)
- linear: RED-4321 (linked above)

If I get one quick win: add a small preprocessor that trims repeated trailing punctuation for short queries and ship as a toggle for hosted-api. Low effort, reduces surface while we dig deeper.

End notes: store a few more raw samples (save to /tmp/trailing_punct_samples.json) and run small annotation round (3 raters) to validate rubric consistency.

-- Mateo
(half-baked, will clean after repro)

source.title: Latency stitch: morning triage + microtasks
source.content:
Morning capture (quick log) - context:
- Noticed steady p99 uplift starting 2024-11-07 02:00 UTC after kernel refactor (PR 1186). Regression is small-ish in average latency (~5-8% overall) but p99 jumps from ~120ms -> 210ms on some instance classes (a100-80gb, mixed NUMA).
- Customer impact: a couple of production routes saw timeouts (10s) sparingly; CS pinged at 03:15 UTC. No full outage.

Observed symptoms (quick bullets):
- p50 ~ unchanged; p95 +10-15%; p99 up to +80% on long-context requests (>1k tokens).
- Spike correlates with high-concurrency bursts and KV cache cold-start windows after node restarts.
- NVLink saturation metrics slightly higher on affected nodes during spikes (GPU -> GPU copy ops up ~30%).
- Per-request kernel stack samples show extra memcpy / staging path not present in baseline (pre-PR).

Immediate hypotheses (ranked):
1) Kernel refactor introduced an extra mem staging step when KV cache miss falls back to model layers, causing serialized cross-GPU copies for long contexts. Explains NVLink bump and p99.
2) NUMA/cuda pinning subtlety: new code path changes thread-to-GPU dispatch ordering so some requests hit non-local memory more often.
3) Batching fallback logic: when sequence length > 1024, fallback to single-GPU slow path due to buffer sizing edge-case added in PR. Fewer tokens batched -> higher tail.

Quick diagnostics to run (high priority):
- Reproduce in perf lab: replay production trace (trace id: prod-20241107-02) with PR branch and main. Compare per-op breakdown.
  Commands: 
  - ./scripts/replay_trace.sh --trace prod-20241107-02 --branch pr-1186 --out /tmp/replay-pr1186.json
  - python tools/latency_breakdown.py /tmp/replay-pr1186.json --op-filter memcpy,cudaMemcpy --aggregate p50,p95,p99
- Capture nvlink counters during synthetic long-ctx burst:
  - nvidia-smi nvlink --query-gpu=tx_bytes,rx_bytes --loop-ms=500 > /tmp/nvlink-pr1186.log  (run baseline and pr branch)
- Check kernel logs for "staging buffer" or "fallback single-gpu" strings (we added logging in PR but might not be hit with current log level).
  - grep -n "staging" /var/log/redwood/runtime/*.log || true

Short-term mitigations (if reproduces):
- Hotfix option A (low-risk): revert the single refactor commit that changed memcpy staging loop (git revert <sha>) and run canary for 2h.
- Hotfix option B (safer): force routing policy for long-context routes to a variant with old kernel (model-variant:v1.8-legacy) until full fix. Update routing TTL short.
- Traffic shaping: temporarily reduce concurrency cap for affected routes via gateway quotas (rate-limited for 15m windows) to lower NVLink pressure.

Experiment matrix (what to test + metrics):
- Baseline (main): run 1000 replayed requests (1k-2k tokens) concurrent=40 -> record p50/p95/p99, nvlink tx/rx, memcpy op time.
- PR branch (pr-1186): same scenario.
- PR branch + cuda-pinning-fix: change THREAD->GPU mapping to previous mapping (env override: RW_THREAD_PIN=legacy) to test hypothesis 2.
- PR branch + batch-size-forcing: set MAX_BATCH_TOKENS=4096 to avoid fallback slow path; see impact on latency and throughput.

Notes for triage meeting (10:30 AM):
- Bring perf graphs: p50/p95/p99 by route for last 24h, NVLink per-node heatmap, GC/CPU spikes.
- Side-by-side waterfall for 3 example requests (one good, two p99) showing op times (model forward, kv-lookup, memcpy).
- Short list of possible hotfixes and estimated RU (risk/urgency): revert commit (~30m deploy), route pin (~15m), traffic cap (~5m).

Actionable TODOs (me):
- [ ] Run replay + op breakdown for PR branch (ETA 11:00) — Mateo
- [ ] Collect nvlink logs on two affected nodes (n5, n7) during synthetic burst — Hector (pinged)
- [ ] Draft rollback PR if results show staging memcpy is culprit — Mateo
- [ ] Create canary rollout plan for revert (2 nodes -> 10% -> 100%) and doc in Confluence — Rina
- [ ] Post initial findings to #runtime-triage with clear next steps & assign owner for hotfix — Ivy (covering oncall)

Helpful commands/snippets (pasteable):
- perf replay: ./scripts/replay_trace.sh --trace prod-20241107-02 --branch pr-1186 --concurrency 40 --samples 1000
- per-op drilldown: python tools/latency_breakdown.py /tmp/replay-pr1186.json --group-by op --percentiles 50,95,99 > /tmp/op-breakdown.txt
- nvlink capture: sudo watch -n 0.5 'nvidia-smi nvlink --query-gpu=tx_bytes,rx_bytes --format=csv'

Notes to self (observations while poking):
- Saw a corner case where kv-cache warmup path allocates host staging buffer of size (seq_len * 4 * sizeof(float)) which is ~10% bigger than pre-refactor. That pushes memory alignment to a different PCIe page boundary -> extra copy path triggered. This could be the golden ticket.
- If true, a minimal fix is to preserve old allocation granularity (pad to 64KB chunks) or reuse slab allocator from earlier commit. Try quick prototype: change allocator chunk to 64k and rerun replay (low-risk local patch).

Meeting follow-ups (post-triage):
- If hotfix applied, monitor p99 on canary and escalate automatic rollback if p99 > baseline + 20% for 15m.
- Add an eval to Optimize that runs synthetic long-ctx burst weekly against new kernel PRs to catch regressions earlier.

Random quick thoughts (brain dump):
- Add a small e2e test that intentionally hits the KV-cache miss path with long keys to validate no extra memcpy is introduced.
- Document memory alignment invariants in developer guide (kernel/alloc.md).
- Consider a lightweight sampling profiler to attach to live requests that hit >350ms to capture op-level stack w/o heavy overhead.

End of morning log — will update as I run replays.

---
Change log: 2024-11-08 created initial triage note.
Change log: 2024-11-10 added allocator hypothesis and commands run notes.
